title: HVAC Systems and Indoor Air Quality: What Every Technician Should Know
description:
Already Purchased This Course?


Access Here

Topics covered include:

    • Introduction to Course
    • Module 1: Understanding the Basics of HVAC and Airflow
    • Module 2: Common HVAC Contaminants and Their Impact
    • Module 3: Filtration and Air Cleaning Technologies
    • Module 4: Ventilation: Fresh Air and IAQ
    • Module 5: HVAC Maintenance and Air Quality Outcomes
    • Module 6: Moisture Control and HVAC Systems
    • Module 7: Ductwork: Inspection, Cleaning, and Sealing
    • Module 8: HVAC and Odour Control
    • Module 9: Communicating IAQ Findings with Clients
    • Module 10: IAQ-Friendly HVAC Upgrades and Recommendations
short_description:
What can you expect from this Course?










his course provides HVAC technicians with essential knowledge and practical skills to assess, maintain, and optimise indoor air quality (IAQ) through HVAC system performance. Covering everything from airflow fundamentals to contaminant control, the course explains how HVAC components can either improve or degrade indoor environments depending on their design and maintenance.
Technicians will learn how to identify common IAQ hazards—such as mould, dust, VOCs, and odours—and explore solutions through proper filtration, ventilation, moisture control, duct maintenance, and HVAC system upgrades. Emphasis is also placed on clear client communication, helping technicians translate technical findings into actionable, relatable advice.
Whether you’re troubleshooting an existing system or recommending upgrades, this course empowers you to deliver healthier, cleaner air and more comfortable living and working conditions for your clients.









Course Duration:
Approx 2 Hours

short_description:
What can you expect from this Course?










This course equips professionals with the knowledge and tools to identify, manage, and reduce indoor dust and particulate pollution across residential, commercial, and restoration environments. Participants will explore how particulates form, how they move, and their potential health impacts—including respiratory, cardiovascular, and cognitive effects.
The course covers measurement technologies like particle counters, filtration methods including HEPA and MERV-rated filters, and effective cleaning strategies to reduce both airborne and settled dust. Learners will also gain insight into HVAC system influence, source control practices, and long-term air quality monitoring.
By the end of the course, technicians will be able to assess indoor particulate levels, recommend targeted interventions, and communicate findings and solutions clearly to clients—promoting healthier, cleaner indoor environments.

Approx 2 Hours

short_description:
What can you expect from this Course?










This course provides restoration and remediation professionals with the practical knowledge needed to effectively use Air Filtration Devices (AFDs), also known as air scrubbers, to improve air quality on job sites. Participants will learn how AFDs function, when and where to deploy them, how to select and manage filters, and how to configure systems for both recirculation and negative pressure environments.
Through detailed modules, the course covers setup strategies, air change calculations (ACH), maintenance routines, and compliance with safety and industry standards. Technicians will gain confidence in monitoring air quality using tools like particle counters and pressure gauges, and in documenting their work clearly for clients and insurers. By the end of the course, professionals will be equipped to use AFDs to reduce airborne contaminants, support health and safety, and enhance the overall quality of their restoration work.









Course Duration:
Approx 2 Hours

title: Introduction to Mould Identification and Remediation
description:
DRAFT — pending SME review. This course content has been drafted against ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 and Australian work health and safety requirements. It must be reviewed and approved by a qualified subject-matter expert before publishing. Course status has been left as draft for that reason.

About this course

Mould is a normal part of the outdoor and indoor environment. It becomes a problem inside buildings only when excess moisture lets it grow and colonise materials. This foundational course teaches you to recognise mould, understand why it grows, and judge when a situation needs professional remediation. It follows the framework of the international mould remediation Standard, ANSI/IICRC S520:2024, and applies it to Australian buildings, climate and regulation. It is the entry point to CARSI’s mould remediation pathway (Level 2 and Level 3) and carries IICRC Continuing Education Credit (CEC) hours.

Who this course is for

Restoration and cleaning technicians, property and facilities managers, building inspectors, pest and building consultants, insurance assessors and anyone who needs to identify mould and moisture problems reliably before deciding on a course of action. No prior mould training is assumed.

Learning objectives

By the end of this course you should be able to:

  • Explain what mould is, how it reproduces, and its role in the built and natural environment.
  • Describe the conditions required for mould growth and why moisture is the factor you control.
  • Summarise the potential health effects of mould exposure and identify susceptible occupants, without making a medical diagnosis.
  • Carry out a structured visual inspection and use moisture and environmental instruments to locate hidden dampness and growth.
  • Explain the purpose and limits of air and surface sampling, including Australian air-sampling practice under AS/NZS 4849.1, and the role of the Indoor Environmental Professional (IEP).
  • Apply the S520 Condition 1, 2 and 3 framework and recognise when remediation is required and when to escalate to a Level 2 or Level 3 remediation.

Course structure

Module 1: What mould is and why it matters

Moulds are fungi that reproduce by releasing microscopic spores which are always present in outdoor and indoor air. When spores land on a damp surface they germinate, grow filaments (hyphae), colonise the material and produce more spores and extracellular material. S520:2024 describes this as actual growth and distinguishes it from settled spores or traces that simply reflect a clean, dry building. Understanding that spores are ubiquitous is the key first lesson: the goal of remediation is never a ‘spore-free’ building, but a return to normal fungal ecology — what S520:2024 defines as Condition 1.

Module 2: The conditions for growth — moisture is the one you control

Mould needs a moisture source, an organic food source, a suitable temperature and time. In an Australian home the food source (paper-faced plasterboard/Gyprock, timber framing, dust, textiles) and temperature are almost always available, so moisture is the controllable variable. Common Australian moisture sources include rising damp and poor sub-floor ventilation in older weatherboard and double-brick homes, roof and flashing leaks, plumbing failures, and condensation on cold surfaces in poorly ventilated bathrooms, wardrobes and bedrooms during humid coastal summers and cold southern winters. S520:2024 §3 (building and material science) explains how moisture moves through wall, floor and ceiling assemblies; if you do not correct the moisture, mould returns.

Module 3: Health effects and susceptible occupants

Exposure to mould can cause allergic and irritant responses, and mould produces microbial volatile organic compounds (the musty odour) and, in some species, mycotoxins. Effects vary widely between individuals. S520:2024 identifies susceptible and immunocompromised people as being at greater risk. Your role is to identify and control the exposure, not to diagnose illness: where occupants report health concerns, refer them to a medical practitioner and document the concern. For plain-language public health guidance use Australian sources such as enHealth and state health department mould fact sheets rather than overseas material.

Module 4: Visual identification and the building inspection

S520:2024 §7 sets out a structured inspection: gather the building history and occupant information, then inspect systematically. Look for visible growth, water staining, efflorescence, peeling paint, cupped or swollen skirting and architraves, and the tell-tale musty odour. In Australian construction pay attention to sub-floor spaces, wall cavities behind Gyprock, the underside of roof sarking, wet-area junctions and behind fixed joinery. Visible mould does not require a laboratory to confirm it is mould before you act on the moisture.

Module 5: Instrument identification

Instruments help you find moisture you cannot see. Use pin and pin-less moisture meters to map damp materials, a thermo-hygrometer to record temperature, relative humidity and dew point, thermal imaging to reveal temperature anomalies from hidden moisture, and a borescope to inspect wall and ceiling cavities. All mains equipment used in Australia operates on 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A general power outlets and must be protected by a residual current device (RCD/safety switch); site leads and equipment should be inspected and tested (tested and tagged) in line with AS/NZS 3760. Record readings in metric units and compare damp areas against a dry reference area in the same building.

Module 6: Sampling basics

Sampling can support an assessment but never replaces finding and fixing the moisture. Air sampling (for example spore-trap cassettes) and surface sampling (tape lift, swab or bulk) are interpreted by a laboratory and, ideally, an Indoor Environmental Professional. In Australia, air sampling is carried out in accordance with AS/NZS 4849.1 for the collection of air samples. Results are comparative — indoor versus outdoor and affected versus unaffected areas — and have real limitations. S520:2024 treats sampling as one input to a preliminary determination, not a pass/fail test on its own.

Module 7: The Condition 1, 2 and 3 framework

S520:2024 classifies indoor environments relative to mould into three conditions. Condition 1 (normal fungal ecology) is a clean, dry indoor environment that may carry settled or airborne spores or traces reflective of that clean, dry state. Condition 3 is actual mould growth — active, dormant, dead, visible or hidden. Condition 2 is an area contaminated with residual mould material (spores, fragments, extracellular material) that has settled from a Condition 3 source in the same environment. The objective of every remediation is to return Condition 2 and Condition 3 areas to Condition 1.

Module 8: When remediation is required — and what comes next

Once you have identified a moisture source and Condition 2 or 3 contamination, S520:2024 §7.4 has you make a preliminary determination and, where remediation is warranted, develop a remediation work plan (§7.5). This foundational course stops at identification and the decision to act. Small, clearly-bounded jobs are addressed in CARSI’s Level 2 course; large, complex or high-risk jobs — and any job needing independent verification — are addressed in Level 3. Knowing who does what (remediator, IEP, and the other materially interested parties such as owners, occupants and insurers) is part of making a sound call.

Standards and references applied

  • ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 — definitions (Condition 1/2/3, actual growth, containment, IEP); §3 building and material science; §7 inspection and preliminary determination.
  • AS/NZS 4849.1 — air sampling (collection of air samples for indoor air quality assessment).
  • AS/NZS 3760 — in-service inspection and testing of electrical equipment (test and tag) for 230 V site equipment.
  • Work Health and Safety Act 2011 and WHS Regulations (Safe Work Australia model laws); New Zealand Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 where operating in NZ.
  • Australian public-health guidance on mould (enHealth and state/territory health departments).

References to ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 in this course paraphrase and apply the Standard for teaching purposes and cite the relevant clauses. They do not reproduce the copyrighted text of the Standard. Practitioners should obtain and read the current edition of the Standard in full.
short_description:
What can you expect from this course? This foundational course gives you a reliable way to identify mould and moisture problems in Australian buildings and to decide when professional remediation is needed. You will learn what mould is, why moisture drives its growth, the potential health effects and who is most at risk, how to inspect visually and with instruments, the basics of air and surface sampling under AS/NZS 4849.1, and the ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 Condition 1/2/3 framework. It is the entry point to CARSI’s Level 2 and Level 3 mould remediation courses

Australian English, metric units and 230 V / 10 A context throughout.

Course duration: approx 1 hour.

short_description:
What is ChatGPT and what are ChatGPT prompts?
 
ChatGPT is an artificial intelligence chatbot developed by OpenAI, it can answer questions and assist you with tasks, such as composing emails, essays, and code.
ChatGPT prompts are short statements or questions provided by users to guide the conversational direction with the language model. These prompts serve as input and help set the context for the model’s responses. Users can use prompts to ask questions, request information, seek assistance, or engage in various conversational topics. By providing a specific prompt, users can direct the conversation and responses tailored to their needs or interests. ChatGPT then generates responses based on the input received, aiming to provide helpful and relevant information or engage in meaningful dialogue.
 
Unlock the full potential of ChatGPT with our comprehensive prompt book for the Cleaning and Restoration Industry, providing you with expertly crafted prompts to engage in insightful conversations, obtain valuable information, and unleash the power of this advanced language model.

title: Level 3 - Mould Remediation
description:
DRAFT — pending SME review. This course content has been drafted against ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 and Australian work health and safety requirements. It must be reviewed and approved by a qualified subject-matter expert before publishing. Course status has been left as draft for that reason.

About this course

This advanced course covers large-area and complex mould remediation — the jobs that exceed a standard residential scope. It builds on CARSI’s Level 2 course and works through full containment with continuous negative pressure, structural and contents remediation, coordination with an Indoor Environmental Professional, independent clearance, hazardous-material interfaces, and the insurer and litigation-grade documentation these projects demand. It applies ANSI/IICRC S520:2024, in particular the Limitations, Complexities, Complications and Conflicts of §8, to difficult Australian scenarios. It carries IICRC Continuing Education Credit (CEC) hours.

Who this course is for

Experienced remediation technicians, supervisors, project managers and estimators responsible for large, complex, commercial or disputed mould projects. Completion of CARSI’s Level 2 course or equivalent competency is assumed.

Learning objectives

By the end of this course you should be able to:

  • Recognise the Limitations, Complexities, Complications and Conflicts (S520:2024 §8) that escalate a job beyond a standard residential remediation.
  • Design full containment with an anteroom, decontamination staging, critical barriers and continuous, monitored negative pressure (S520:2024 §9.2, §9.3.3–§9.3.6).
  • Manage structural remediation of large-area, hidden and systemic contamination and the safe disposal of contaminated materials (S520:2024 §9.3.7–§9.3.11).
  • Manage contents remediation, inventory and pack-out, and make restorability decisions including for high-value and irreplaceable items (S520:2024 §11).
  • Coordinate the Indoor Environmental Professional and independent post-remediation verification and clearance (S520:2024 §7.1, §12).
  • Manage HVAC contamination in larger and commercial buildings (S520:2024 §10; AS 1668.2).
  • Identify hazardous and regulated material interfaces — asbestos, lead and silica — and stop work appropriately (S520:2024 §8.5, §8.9) within the Australian licensing framework.
  • Produce insurer-grade and litigation-grade documentation, including scope, deviations, change orders and records retention (S520:2024 §6.3, §6.6, §1.2).

Course structure

Module 1: When a job becomes Level 3

S520:2024 §8 recognises that real projects carry Limitations (§8.1), Complexities (§8.2), Complications (§8.3) and Conflicts (§8.4). A job escalates to Level 3 when the contaminated area is large, when contamination is hidden or systemic through cavities and multiple rooms, when occupants are susceptible or highly sensitised, when there is a Category 3 (grossly contaminated) water or sewage interface, or when the work is disputed. Recognising escalation early — and resourcing the job for it — is the difference between a controlled project and a cross-contamination event.

Module 2: Full containment and decontamination

Complex jobs use full containment (§9.2, §9.3.3): a sealed work area built with critical barriers over openings, an anteroom or decontamination chamber for entry and exit, and continuous negative pressure maintained by HEPA air filtration devices. Pressure differential should be monitored and logged (for example with a manometer) so you can prove containment held for the duration of the works. Personnel suit up and enter, and follow a defined containment exit protocol to avoid tracking contamination out (§9.3.6, §9.3.10). Signage marks the containment (§9.3.4).

Module 3: Structural remediation at scale

Large-area structural remediation (§9.3.7–§9.3.11) means removing affected finishes and, where needed, opening assemblies to reach hidden growth: wall cavities behind Gyprock, the sub-floor, the roof void and behind sarking are common hiding places in Australian construction, and double-brick cavities and party walls (§8.6 common walls) create their own access and cross-contamination challenges. Contaminated material is bagged and disposed of appropriately (§9.3.11). Throughout, the goal remains a physical return of the structure to Condition 1.

Module 4: Contents remediation and pack-out

S520:2024 §11 governs contents. Inspect and evaluate each item for restorability (§11.1), inventory and pack contents removed from affected areas (§11.2), and choose a cleaning location and method (§11.3). High-value and irreplaceable items — artworks, documents, heirlooms — need special care and sometimes specialist referral (§11.4), while unrestorable items are documented and dispositioned (§11.5). A clear inventory protects both occupant and remediator and underpins any insurance claim.

Module 5: HVAC in complex and commercial buildings

In larger buildings the HVAC system is both a contamination pathway and a remediation challenge (S520:2024 §10). Ducted and central systems can distribute spores building-wide, so isolation, assessment and, where required, cleaning of the system are part of the scope. Australian mechanical ventilation is designed to AS 1668.2; coordinate with the building’s mechanical services and manage airflow and pressure so the system does not defeat containment (§10.2.3).

Module 6: The IEP and independent verification

On complex and disputed jobs, an Indoor Environmental Professional is engaged to assess the project and to perform independent post-remediation verification (§7.1, §12). The principle of independence matters: the party who performed the remediation should not be the party who clears it. Verification confirms the return to Condition 1 through visual assessment, moisture readings and, where warranted, air and surface sampling under AS/NZS 4849.1, interpreted against outdoor and unaffected-area baselines.

Module 7: Hazardous and regulated materials

S520:2024 §8.5 flags that hazardous and regulated materials are outside the scope of mould remediation and must be handled under their own regimes. In Australian buildings constructed or renovated before 2004, assume asbestos-containing materials may be present (fibre-cement sheet, vinyl backing, textured coatings) and manage lead-based paint and respirable crystalline silica risks. Where these are disturbed you must stop mould work and engage licensed removalists under the relevant WHS asbestos and hazardous-substances regulations before continuing (§8.9 work stoppage).

Module 8: Insurer, regulatory and litigation documentation

Complex mould jobs are frequently reviewed by insurers, regulators or courts, so documentation is a deliverable in its own right (§1.2, §6.3). Maintain the written scope and remediation work plan, photographic evidence, pressure and moisture logs, waste disposal records, change orders (§8.7), and a clear record of any deviation from the Standard with its rationale (§6.3.4). Keep records for the required retention period (§6.4) and manage your own risk and insurance position (§6.6). Contemporaneous, well-organised documentation is the single best defence if a project is later challenged.

Module 9: Managing materially interested parties and conflict

Large projects involve owners, occupants, insurers, hygienists and other trades, whose interests can conflict (§8.4). Communicate health concerns and process honestly (§6.2), manage expectations about what verification can and cannot prove, and keep decisions and approvals in writing. Clear communication prevents most disputes and contains the ones that still arise.

Module 10: Project close-out

Close out on independent verification confirming Condition 1, a documented reoccupancy clearance, a complete records package handed to the client, and records retained per §6.4. A Level 3 project is not finished when the mould is gone — it is finished when the return to Condition 1 is verified and the evidence trail is complete.

Standards and references applied

  • ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 — §1 principles; §6 administrative procedures, documentation, records retention and insurance; §7 inspection and IEP qualifications; §8 Limitations, Complexities, Complications and Conflicts (including §8.5 hazardous/regulated materials, §8.6 common walls, §8.7 change orders, §8.9 work stoppage); §9 structural remediation (engineering controls and work procedures); §10 HVAC; §11 contents remediation; §12 post remediation verification.
  • AS/NZS 4849.1 — air sampling for indoor air quality assessment.
  • AS 1668.2 — mechanical ventilation in buildings.
  • AS/NZS 1715 / AS/NZS 1716 — respiratory protective equipment.
  • Work Health and Safety Act 2011 and WHS Regulations, including the asbestos and hazardous-substances provisions and licensed asbestos removal; NZ Health and Safety at Work Act 2015.

References to ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 in this course paraphrase and apply the Standard for teaching purposes and cite the relevant clauses. They do not reproduce the copyrighted text of the Standard. Practitioners should obtain and read the current edition of the Standard in full.
short_description:

title: Level 2 - Mould Remediation
description:
DRAFT — pending SME review. This course content has been drafted against ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 and Australian work health and safety requirements. It must be reviewed and approved by a qualified subject-matter expert before publishing. Course status has been left as draft for that reason.

About this course

This standard remediation course takes you from identifying mould to safely remediating a typical residential Australian job. It follows the process and principles of ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 — safety, documentation, contamination control, contamination removal and moisture correction — and applies them under Australian work health and safety law. It assumes you have completed CARSI’s Introduction to Mould Identification, or have equivalent knowledge, and it prepares you for the complex and large-loss work covered in Level 3. It carries IICRC Continuing Education Credit (CEC) hours.

Who this course is for

Restoration and remediation technicians, cleaning and water-damage operators, and supervisors who carry out or oversee residential-scale mould remediation. A working understanding of mould identification and the Condition 1/2/3 framework is assumed.

Learning objectives

By the end of this course you should be able to:

  • Apply the five principles of S520:2024 §1 to a residential remediation from start to finish.
  • Assess a job and develop a documented remediation work plan and scope (S520:2024 §7).
  • Select and use respiratory and personal protective equipment under AS/NZS 1715 and AS/NZS 1716 and control site risks under Australian WHS law.
  • Build source and limited containment and establish negative air pressure using an air filtration device (S520:2024 §9.1–§9.2).
  • Physically remove the mould source and contaminated porous materials with effective dust control (S520:2024 §1.4, §9.3.7).
  • Apply HEPA vacuuming and damp cleaning methods correctly, and understand the limited role of antimicrobials and coatings (S520:2024 §9.3.8, §2).
  • Manage the HVAC system and dry the structure so the moisture problem does not recur (S520:2024 §10, §1.5).
  • Carry out a post-remediation evaluation and verification against a return to Condition 1, and document and hand over the job (S520:2024 §9.3.12–§9.3.13, §12, §6.3).

Course structure

Module 1: From identification to action — the five principles

S520:2024 §1 sets out five principles that govern every job: provide for the safety and health of workers and occupants (§1.1); document the conditions and work processes (§1.2); control the contamination so it does not spread (§1.3); physically remove the contamination (§1.4); and prevent recontamination by correcting the moisture (§1.5). Everything in this course hangs off these five principles. This module recaps the Condition 1/2/3 framework and frames the residential job around them.

Module 2: Safety, health and Australian WHS

Under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 and WHS Regulations you must manage risks so far as is reasonably practicable, following the hierarchy of controls. For mould work this means respiratory protection — typically a P2 (or P3) respirator selected, fit-checked and maintained under AS/NZS 1715 and manufactured to AS/NZS 1716 — plus eye and skin protection and disposable coveralls. Electrical safety matters on damp sites: 230 V / 10 A equipment must run through an RCD/safety switch and leads should be tested and tagged (AS/NZS 3760). Record your risk assessment and safe work method statement. In New Zealand the equivalent duty sits under the Health and Safety at Work Act 2015.

Module 3: Assessment and the remediation work plan

Following S520:2024 §7.3–§7.5, inspect the site, establish the extent of Condition 2 and 3 contamination and the moisture source, then write a remediation work plan and scope. Identify the materially interested parties — owner, occupants, insurer — and the documentation you will keep (§1.2, §6.3). A clear written scope protects both the occupant and the remediator and is the reference against which the job is later verified.

Module 4: Containment and engineering controls

S520:2024 §9.2 requires engineering controls to stop cross-contamination from affected to unaffected areas. For a typical residential job this is source or limited containment using polyethylene sheeting (commonly 6 mil / about 0.15 mm), sealed to create a work area, combined with an air filtration device (§9.1.3) exhausted to establish negative air pressure inside the containment. Negative pressure keeps contaminated air moving into the work area, not out into the home. Plan containment access and keep the containment maintained throughout the works (§9.3.3, §9.3.5).

Module 5: Source removal

The heart of remediation is physically removing the mould, not treating it in place (§1.4). Porous materials with actual growth — paper-faced Gyprock, saturated batt insulation, mould-affected carpet and underlay, water-damaged timber-based sheet products — are generally removed and disposed of. Control dust while you work: mist to suppress airborne particulate where appropriate (§2.1.3), work methodically, and keep contaminated material bagged. Removal is done inside the containment with the AFD running.

Module 6: Cleaning methods

Non-porous and semi-porous surfaces and structural framing that can be saved are cleaned rather than removed (§9.3.8). The core methods are HEPA vacuuming (§9.1.5) to capture settled spores and debris, followed by damp wiping with detergent solution to physically remove residue. Antimicrobials and fungicidal or mould-resistant coatings have a limited, defined role and are never a substitute for physical removal or for correcting moisture (§2.1.2, §2.2). Painting over mould is not remediation.

Module 7: HVAC considerations

If a heating, ventilation and air-conditioning system served the affected area it can spread spores through the building. Following S520:2024 §10.2, isolate or shut the system down during the works, protect or seal returns and supplies within the containment, and assess whether the system itself (for example a reverse-cycle split or ducted system) has been contaminated and needs cleaning. Manage airflow and pressure so the HVAC does not defeat your containment (§10.2.3).

Module 8: Drying and moisture correction

Principle §1.5 — prevent recontamination — is delivered by correcting the moisture that caused the growth. Fix the leak, improve sub-floor or bathroom ventilation, address condensation, and then dry the structure to a stable, dry equilibrium, verified with moisture meters against a dry reference. Basic psychrometry (temperature, relative humidity, dew point, and specific humidity) tells you whether your drying is working. If the moisture source is not corrected, the mould will return regardless of how well the area was cleaned.

Module 9: Post-remediation evaluation and verification

Before demobilising, carry out a post-remediation evaluation (§9.3.12): the work area should look and smell clean, be visibly free of mould and dust, and be dry. Post-remediation verification (§9.3.13, §12) confirms the return to Condition 1. On many residential jobs a visual and moisture verification is appropriate; where an occupant is susceptible, where there was a dispute, or where the scope calls for it, independent verification by an Indoor Environmental Professional — who should not be the remediator who did the work — provides an arm’s-length clearance, which may include air sampling under AS/NZS 4849.1.

Module 10: Documentation and handover

Close the job with the documentation S520:2024 §6.3 expects: the scope, before-and-after photographs, moisture logs, the containment and equipment used, any deviations from the plan and the reason for them (§6.3.4), and the verification result. Communicate clearly with the occupant about what was done and what they should watch for (§6.2). Good documentation is what turns a completed job into a defensible one if it is ever questioned by an insurer or occupant.

Standards and references applied

  • ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 — §1 principles of mould remediation; §2 chemicals, antimicrobials and coatings; §6 administrative procedures and documentation; §7 inspection and work planning; §9 structural remediation (equipment, engineering controls, work procedures); §10 HVAC; §12 post remediation verification.
  • AS/NZS 1715 (selection, use and maintenance of respiratory protective equipment) and AS/NZS 1716 (respiratory protective devices).
  • AS/NZS 4849.1 — air sampling for indoor air quality assessment.
  • AS/NZS 3760 — in-service inspection and testing of 230 V electrical equipment.
  • Work Health and Safety Act 2011 and WHS Regulations, and the Safe Work Australia model Code of Practice for managing WHS risks; NZ Health and Safety at Work Act 2015.

References to ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 in this course paraphrase and apply the Standard for teaching purposes and cite the relevant clauses. They do not reproduce the copyrighted text of the Standard. Practitioners should obtain and read the current edition of the Standard in full.
short_description:
What can you expect from this course? This standard remediation course follows on from mould identification and teaches you to safely remediate a typical residential Australian mould job using the ANSI/IICRC S520:2024 process: safety and WHS, assessment and work planning, containment and negative air pressure, source removal, HEPA and damp-wipe cleaning, HVAC management, structural drying and moisture correction, and post-remediation verification against a return to Condition 1. Personal protective equipment is covered under AS/NZS 1715 and AS/NZS 1716.

Australian English, metric units, 230 V / 10 A and AS/NZS context throughout.
